decks.description: This deck maximizes self-mill synergy with a genuine graveyard-recursion engine anchored by Liliana, Death's Majesty, whose +1 mills two cards while making tokens, fueling her own -3 which reanimates whatever hits the yard as a Zombie. Stitcher's Supplier and Crow of Dark Tidings both mill aggressively on entering and dying, filling the graveyard for Liliana, Ghoulraiser (which randomly returns a Zombie card to hand), and Cemetery Recruitment. Returned Reveler's death trigger mills both players, adding even more fuel. Deadly Plot again offers flexible removal or Zombie reanimation, and Lord of the Accursed anthem-buffs the team while granting menace. The deck's plan is to mill itself into a graveyard full of threats, then use Liliana and the various recursion pieces to cheat those threats back, eventually closing with her ultimate wiping non-Zombies.
tips: Mill aggressively early with Crow/Supplier
Use Liliana -3 to reanimate best hit
Ghoulraiser fetches random Zombie
Deadly Plot as removal or reanimation
Push Lord of the Accursed menace attacks

decks.description: This build focuses on going wide with Zombie tokens and scaling anthem effects. Endless Ranks of the Dead creates free tokens each upkeep scaling off your existing Zombie count, while Graf Harvest grants the whole team menace and can also exile graveyard creatures to make more tokens. Maalfeld Twins turns its own death into two more bodies, and Gavony Unhallowed grows a permanent counter every time another creature you control dies, meaning sacrifice fodder from Gnawing Zombie (which drains life on sacrifice) directly feeds Gavony Unhallowed's counters. Crow of Dark Tidings and Mire Triton fill the graveyard, supplying targets for Suspicious Shambler's token-making exile ability, Deadly Plot's reanimation mode, and Graf Harvest's exile ability. Lord of the Accursed buffs the team's stats and adds menace redundancy, letting a wide, growing Zombie board push through blockers.
tips: Sacrifice fodder into Gnawing Zombie for Gavony Unhallowed counters
Let Endless Ranks snowball token count
Graf Harvest and Lord of the Accursed stack menace
Exile graveyard creatures for tokens
Use Suspicious Shambler as a late token burst

decks.description: This deck's main engine is Undead Augur, which turns every Zombie death—yours or from Maalfeld Twins' own death trigger, which creates two more Zombies—into a card draw, so trading tokens or chump blockers directly refills your hand. Liliana's Mastery is a strong go-wide payoff, entering with two 2/2 Zombie tokens and permanently pumping the whole team, while Lord of the Accursed adds a second anthem plus a menace-granting activated ability to push damage through. Crow of Dark Tidings mills to fuel Cemetery Recruitment and Deadly Plot's reanimation mode, and Cellar Door slowly generates extra tokens by milling opponents' libraries over time. Marauding Boneslasher's restriction (can't block without another Zombie) is easily met once the board fills up with tokens, making this a wide aggressive Zombie swarm with built-in card advantage.
tips: Trade Zombies into Undead Augur draws
Cast Liliana's Mastery for instant board plus anthem
Menace via Lord of the Accursed to push damage
Reanimate Zombies with Deadly Plot
Let Cellar Door slowly generate extra tokens

decks.description: This is a sacrifice-and-attrition black deck built around punishing opponents for board wipes and edicts. Massacre Wurm delivers a one-sided -2/-2 sweep on entry and then drains 2 life for every creature that dies to it, turning a single card into a blowout. Plaguecrafter forces both players to sacrifice a creature or planeswalker (or discard), which pairs with Massacre Wurm's death-trigger drain if timed after the Wurm resolves, and also feeds Devouring Swarm, which can sacrifice any creature (including tokens from other effects) to pump itself. Eaten Alive offers flexible exile removal paid for either with a sacrificed creature or straight mana, while Durable Coilbug provides cheap recursive sacrifice fodder by returning itself from the graveyard. Yargle, Glutton of Urborg is a brute-force finisher once the board is cleared, and Infernal Idol converts itself into card draw late game.
tips: Sequence Massacre Wurm before Plaguecrafter for extra drain
Use Durable Coilbug as recurring sac fodder
Eaten Alive via sacrifice to save mana
Devouring Swarm grows off any sacrifice
Yargle finishes once board is clear

decks.description: This deck is built entirely around a toolbox of sacrifice-fueled removal creatures rather than one flashy combo. Phyrexian Plaguelord can sacrifice itself for a big -4/-4 or sacrifice other creatures repeatedly for smaller -1/-1 effects, Phyrexian Debaser and Devouring Swarm both offer sacrifice-activated abilities (shrink a creature or pump itself), and Plaguecrafter forces sacrifices from both players, generating fodder for these effects. Deathbloom Thallid and Fungal Infection replace themselves with a 1/1 Saproling token, giving you renewable sacrifice fodder to feed the sac-outlets without losing board presence. Phyrexian Reclamation and Durable Coilbug both offer recursive value, letting you rebuy creatures from the graveyard to keep the sacrifice engine running, while Blighted Fen and Eaten Alive round out the removal suite. Overall this is a grindy attrition deck that trades resources efficiently rather than relying on any single combo piece.
tips: Feed Saproling tokens into sac outlets
Use Plaguecrafter to force awkward sacrifices
Recur creatures with Phyrexian Reclamation
Save Phyrexian Plaguelord's -4/-4 for big threats
Grind opponent out with repeated -1/-1 effects

decks.description: This mono-black deck is built around a sacrifice/aristocrats shell that grinds down opponents through repeated discard, forced sacrifice, and drain effects rather than raw combat power. Plaguecrafter and Skullslither Worm punish opponents by forcing sacrifices or discards the moment they land, while Devouring Swarm and Vermin Gorger-style sac outlets turn expendable bodies into damage or stat boosts. Cards like Durable Coilbug and Kraul Swarm can be recurred from the graveyard to feed the sacrifice engine again and again, and Infernal Idol converts itself into two cards late game. Deathbringer Regent acts as a surprise board wipe if you can stall to five or more creatures, while Eaten Alive offers cheap exile removal if you're willing to feed it a body. The overall plan is attrition: keep opponents short on resources and creatures while your own recursive threats keep coming back.
tips: Feed sac outlets with recurring bodies
Coilbug and Kraul Swarm rebuy from grave
Time Plaguecrafter for max damage
Hold Deathbringer Regent for wipe
Eaten Alive for cheap exile

decks.description: Another mono-black sacrifice deck, this build leans even harder into the aristocrats plan with multiple dedicated sac-payoffs. Vermin Gorger and Witch's Cauldron both turn a sacrificed creature into direct value (life drain or card draw), while Swarm of Bloodflies grows every time any creature dies anywhere, making it a scaling flying threat that gets better as the game grinds on. Plaguecrafter and Skullslither Worm again apply early disruption by forcing sacrifices or discards, and Ophiomancer provides a renewable stream of deathtouch Snake tokens that are perfect fodder for the sacrifice outlets. Durable Coilbug offers recursion to keep feeding the engine, and Eaten Alive gives you a way to remove a problematic threat by paying the sacrifice tax. The deck wants to trade small creatures for value and big life swings while Swarm of Bloodflies and Blight Keeper close out the game.
tips: Ophiomancer tokens feed sac outlets
Bloodflies grows off any death
Gorger and Cauldron convert sac to value
Recur Durable Coilbug for fodder
Force sacs with Plaguecrafter early

decks.description: This mono-red deck is a pure haste-aggro list designed to dump the whole hand onto the board and swing immediately, since nearly every creature has haste. Ardoz, Cobbler of War pumps every creature that enters (including itself) by +2/+0 for the turn, meaning any creature you flash into play, cast, or make via Ardoz's own token ability hits significantly harder that turn. Bolt Hound's attack trigger buffs the rest of the team, stacking with Ardoz to push through big alpha strikes, while Markov Warlord and Swift Kick clear blockers out of the way. Warcry Phoenix rewards attacking wide with three or more creatures by letting you pay to bring it back tapped and attacking, and Kiki-Jiki can copy any nonlegendary hasty creature for an extra hasty attacker that turn. The gameplan is simple: flood the board fast, buff with Ardoz and Bolt Hound, remove blockers, and race before the opponent stabilizes.
tips: Play everything for immediate haste swings
Ardoz buffs every ETB that turn
Attack with 3+ for Warcry Phoenix
Use Markov Warlord/Swift Kick to clear blockers
Kiki-Jiki copies hasty attackers

decks.description: This is a masochistic mono-red burn deck that intentionally deals damage to its own controller in exchange for extra reach and value, betting that it can race the opponent down before its self-inflicted damage becomes fatal. Auntie Blyte, Bad Influence turns every point of self-damage from cards like Fireslinger, Goblin Artillery, Ravenous Giant, and Smoldering Efreet into +1/+1 counters that can later be converted into direct damage to any target, effectively recycling your own pain into reach. Coalhauler Swine and Spear Spewer act as symmetric damage engines that hit both players, and Impending Doom forces a creature to attack every turn while dealing a burst of damage to its controller when it dies. The deck's core interaction is feeding Auntie Blyte with all the incidental self-damage sources so she becomes a growing finisher/removal machine, while Loxodon Warhammer and direct burn spells like Sarkhan's Rage close out the race.
tips: Feed Auntie Blyte with self-damage
Use counters as reach/removal
Race, don't grind
Watch your own life total
Warhammer for lifelink stability

decks.description: This mono-red Dragons deck blends a tribal payoff shell with a planeswalker that can turn into a Dragon itself. Sarkhan, the Dragonspeaker can become a 4/4 flying indestructible haste Dragon on demand, and every time you activate one of his abilities, Sarkhan's Whelp pings a target for 1 damage, turning his loyalty abilities into extra reach. Kargan Dragonrider only has flying while you control a Dragon, so the deck wants to keep Dragon bodies like Dragon Egg's token, Furnace Whelp, or Bogardan Dragonheart's transformation online to enable it. Dragon Mage offers a symmetrical but powerful hand-refill effect on combat damage, while Dragon Fodder and Tormenting Voice smooth the curve with tokens and card draw. The plan is to ramp into Dragon threats, use Sarkhan as both removal (-3) and a beefy attacker, and grind out card advantage through looting effects.
tips: Sarkhan abilities ping via Whelp
Keep a Dragon out for Kargan flying
Use Sarkhan -3 as removal
Dragon Mage refill both hands
Bogardan Dragonheart sac fodder for a Dragon

decks.description: This mono-red Dragons deck is built to cheat out and chain big flying Dragons as fast as possible. Dragonlord's Servant and Dragonspeaker Shaman both reduce the cost of Dragon spells, letting you jump ahead on curve into Lathliss, Dragon Queen, whose ability creates a 5/5 flying Dragon token every time another nontoken Dragon enters — meaning Sparktongue Dragon, Dragon Mage, or any other Dragon you cast after Lathliss nets you a bonus 5/5 body for free. Dragon's Hoard ramps mana of any color while banking gold counters off each Dragon that enters, letting you convert those counters into card draw later. Kargan Dragonrider gains flying as long as you control any Dragon, turning it into an evasive threat once the Dragon count builds. The deck's core combo is simply resolving Lathliss and then deploying more Dragons to flood the board with 5/5 fliers.
tips: Land Lathliss before more Dragons
Cost reducers accelerate the curve
Every Dragon after Lathliss makes a 5/5
Hoard counters into card draw
Kargan flies once any Dragon is out

decks.description: This mono-red cycling deck cares as much about discarding cards for value as casting them, using cycling as a second mode on almost every card. Drannith Stinger pings opponents every time you cycle another card, Prickly Marmoset and Flameblade Adept both grow when you cycle or discard, and Reptilian Reflection can turn into a 5/4 hasty trampler for a turn whenever you cycle anything, making it a surprise finisher. Rooting Moloch lets you reclaim a cycled card from your graveyard to effectively cycle it twice, and several spells like Deem Worthy and Quakefoot Cyclops have bonus effects when cycled rather than cast (dealing 2 damage or removing a blocker). Yidaro, Wandering Monster is built for the long game of repeated cycling, eventually cheating itself onto the battlefield from the graveyard after being cycled four times. The deck wants to cycle aggressively early to filter toward action while triggering its payoffs, then turn those cycling triggers into damage and attackers.
tips: Cycle everything for triggers
Stinger and Marmoset reward cycling
Reptilian Reflection becomes a 5/4 attacker

decks.description: This mono-red cycling deck shifts focus toward 'second card each turn' triggers alongside its cycling package, since cycling a card counts as drawing a card for the turn. Irencrag Pyromancer, Mad Ratter, and Bloodhaze Wolverine all care about drawing a second card in a turn, so cycling a cheap card like Forgotten Cave or Go for Blood after your normal draw step turns on Pyromancer's 3 damage, Mad Ratter's two Rat tokens, and Wolverine's first strike pump in one action. Drannith Stinger and Prickly Marmoset layer on more direct cycling payoffs, pinging opponents or growing on every cycle, while Reptilian Reflection again threatens to become a 5/4 hasty attacker. Starstorm doubles as a board wipe or a cheap cycle depending on the situation, giving the deck flexibility between grinding value and clearing the board. The overall plan is to chain cheap cycling cards into a single turn to maximize simultaneous triggers from the second-card and cycling payoffs.
tips: Cycle after your draw for second-card triggers
Pyromancer and Mad Ratter reward extra draws

decks.description: This Izzet-colored (mostly mono-red with a blue splash via Mizzix) spells deck wants to chain cheap instants and sorceries, especially ones with flashback, to trigger a suite of payoff creatures. Young Pyromancer and Spellgorger Weird both reward every spell cast with a token or a +1/+1 counter, while Thermo-Alchemist pings opponents and untaps itself every time you cast an instant or sorcery, letting it fire repeatedly in a single turn if you chain spells. Mizzix, Replica Rider lets you pay {1}{U/R} to copy any spell cast from outside your hand, meaning flashback spells like Electric Revelation, Ignite the Future, Seize the Storm, or Firebolt can be copied for double value when recast from the graveyard. Electric Revelation and Ignite the Future refuel your hand and graveyard so you have more flashback fodder, and Seize the Storm scales directly with how many instants/sorceries are in your graveyard plus flashback cards in exile, rewarding a long grindy spells game. The deck's engine is casting and recasting cheap spells to keep Thermo-Alchemist and Young Pyromancer online while Mizzix doubles up on graveyard recursion.
tips: Chain spells to untap Thermo-Alchemist
Mizzix copies flashback spells
Discard for Electric Revelation/Lightning Axe

decks.description: A leaner version of the Izzet spells archetype, this deck again uses Mizzix, Replica Rider to copy spells cast from outside the hand, pairing well with Act on Impulse and Spark of Creativity, which exile and let you play cards from the top of your library — copying either lets you potentially cast two extra spells off the same trigger. Thermo-Alchemist untaps and pings whenever you cast an instant or sorcery, so cheap burn like Firebolt and Electrify are meant to be recast repeatedly for value and reach. Cyclops Electromancer punishes opponents on entry for X damage equal to instants/sorceries already in your graveyard, rewarding you for having spent your spells earlier in the game, while Sparkmage Apprentice and Grotag Night-Runner add small incidental damage and card advantage. Electric Revelation's flashback lets you loot again later in the game, keeping the deck's burn and card-selection engine running even after the initial hand is spent. Overall this is a tempo-oriented burn/spells shell aiming to chip away at life totals with repeatable pings while occasionally getting a bonus copy off Mizzix.
tips: Mizzix copies exiled/played spells

Recast Electric Revelation for late fuel

decks.description: This deck's game plan revolves around flooding the board with Treasure tokens and then turning that artifact glut into damage and mana acceleration. Rapacious Dragon, Brazen Freebooter, and Captain Lannery Storm all pump out Treasures on ETB or attack triggers, while Sudden Breakthrough and Trove of Temptation add even more. Those Treasures fuel Welding Sparks, which deals 3 plus your artifact count in damage, turning a simple burn spell into a game-ending removal tool once you've got a few Treasures out. Sokenzan Smelter converts a spare artifact into a hasty 3/1 Construct each combat, and Blood Aspirant grows every time you sacrifice something, rewarding you for cashing in Treasures and creatures alike. Goldvein Pick keeps the Treasure engine rolling from combat damage, and Dutiful Replicator can literally copy a Treasure token, doubling your available mana in a pinch. Overall this is a sacrifice-fueled aggro-value deck that wants to go wide with cheap artifact-makers, then cash them in for burn, mana, or bodies.
tips: Hold Welding Sparks for max artifacts
Sac Treasures into Sokenzan Smelter
Feed Blood Aspirant sacrifices
Copy a Treasure with Dutiful Replicator
Equip Goldvein Pick early

decks.description: This deck leans harder into the sacrifice-for-damage subtheme than a pure ramp Treasure deck, using artifacts as fuel for direct pings and card advantage rather than just mana. Reckless Fireweaver deals 1 damage to each opponent every time an artifact enters, so every Treasure from Rapacious Dragon, Brazen Freebooter, or Improvised Weaponry becomes a free burn trigger, and Barrage Ogre turns any spare artifact into 2 damage to any target on demand. Professional Face-Breaker generates Treasures off combat damage and lets you sacrifice them to dig for extra cards, while Pyre-Sledge Arsonist scales its damage with however many permanents you've sacrificed that turn, rewarding a big sacrifice-heavy turn. Blood Aspirant again grows from any sacrifice, giving you a threat that gets bigger as you burn through your own artifacts and creatures. The overall plan is an artifact-sacrifice burn deck that chips away at the opponent's life total through incidental triggers as much as combat damage.
tips: Trigger Reckless Fireweaver often
Stack sacrifices before Pyre-Sledge Arsonist
Use Barrage Ogre for reach
Face-Breaker Treasures dig for cards
Grow Blood Aspirant with sacrifices

decks.description: This build is the most mana-hungry and payoff-driven of the Treasure decks, centered on Goldspan Dragon, which not only creates a Treasure whenever it attacks or is targeted but doubles the value of every Treasure you control to two mana each. That effectively lets you flood mana quickly and cast oversized spells like Big Score (draw two and make two Treasures) or Blaze for a huge X. Kuldotha Flamefiend turns a spare artifact into 4 damage split among any number of targets, giving you a flexible removal/burn tool fed directly by your Treasure-makers like Rapacious Dragon and Brazen Freebooter. Vault Robber recycles graveyard creatures into more Treasures, keeping the mana engine going even in a grindy game, and Blood Aspirant again scales off any sacrifice. This is fundamentally a ramp-into-burn deck: generate a critical mass of Treasures, let Goldspan Dragon double their value, then dump that mana into big X-spells or Welding Sparks scaled off your artifact count.
tips: Attack with Goldspan Dragon for Treasures
Double Treasure mana with Goldspan
Dump mana into Blaze/Big Score
Recur creatures with Vault Robber
Sac spare artifacts to Kuldotha Flamefiend

decks.description: This version emphasizes attrition and value from creatures dying, built around Gadrak, the Crown-Scourge, which creates a Treasure for every nontoken creature that died that turn but can only attack once you already control four or more artifacts. Trove of Temptation forces opponents into combat and drips out a Treasure every end step, helping hit that artifact threshold, while Gleaming Barrier is a cheap defensive blocker that also leaves behind a Treasure when it dies, feeding both Gadrak and Blood Aspirant's counter-growth. Kuldotha Flamefiend again offers a sacrifice-fueled 4-damage burst, and Welding Sparks scales with your artifact count just like in the other builds. The deck wants to grind out combat, let creatures trade or die, stack up Treasures from those deaths and its token-makers like Rapacious Dragon and Brazen Freebooter, then unleash Gadrak as a flying finisher once the artifact count is high enough.
tips: Chump block to trigger Gadrak
Hit four artifacts before attacking Gadrak
Use Gleaming Barrier as sac fodder
Scale Welding Sparks with artifacts
Grow Blood Aspirant via sacrifices

decks.description: This deck is built around 'noncombat damage matters' synergies, where cheap burn spells and pinger creatures don't just deal damage but actively pump your team. Chandra's Spitfire gets +3/+0 whenever an opponent takes noncombat damage, and Chandra's Pyreling gets +1/+0 and double strike from the same trigger, so any burn spell or activated damage ability doubles as a combat trick. Wildfire Elemental extends that bonus to your whole board, meaning a single Pillar of Flame or Chandra's Pyrohelix to the opponent's face can turn a mediocre attack into a lethal swing. Chandra, Flame's Fury and Chandra's Magmutt provide repeatable reach, and Fiery Intervention doubles as removal or artifact hate. The overall plan is to chip in noncombat damage every turn to snowball your creatures' stats before attacking for a big finish.
tips: Ping face before attacking
Sequence burn to buff Spitfire/Pyreling
Wildfire Elemental pumps whole team
Save Chandra ultimate for finisher
Use Fiery Intervention on artifacts too

decks.description: Like the other Fiery decks, this build wants to trigger 'noncombat damage to opponent' as often as possible to snowball Chandra's Spitfire and Chandra's Pyreling, but here the enabler is Blisterspit Gremlin, which pings each opponent for 1 and untaps whenever you cast a noncreature spell — letting it fire multiple times per turn if you chain instants and sorceries. Magmatic Channeler rewards a graveyard full of instants/sorceries with +3/+1 and lets you discard to dig two cards deep for more burn, fitting a deck packed with cheap spells like Pillar of Flame, Flames of the Firebrand, and Thrill of Possibility. Wildfire Elemental again turns every ping into a team-wide pump, and Banefire provides a scalable finisher that becomes uncounterable and unpreventable at X=5. This is a spells-matter burn deck that wants to chain cheap instants and sorceries to repeatedly trigger Blisterspit Gremlin and its damage-based pump effects before closing with a big X spell.
tips: Chain spells to untap Blisterspit Gremlin
Fill graveyard for Magmatic Channeler
Ping opponent to buff Spitfire/Pyreling
Save Banefire as finisher
Discard extras to Thrill of Possibility

decks.description: This build doubles down on getting extra value out of its burn spells rather than just casting them once. Ogre Battlecaster lets you recast an instant or sorcery from your graveyard whenever it attacks (paying RR and exiling it after), growing itself by that spell's mana value, so a spell like Flame Lash or Hungry Flames can both deal damage twice and pump your attacker. As with the other Fiery decks, Chandra's Spitfire and Chandra's Pyreling snowball off any noncombat damage to the opponent, and Wildfire Elemental spreads that bonus across the whole team, so Pillar of Flame or Hungry Flames to the face is doing double duty as removal/reach and a combat trick. Thermo-Alchemist offers a repeatable free ping every turn that also feeds the Spitfire/Pyreling/Wildfire package, and Dance with Devils adds sacrificial 1/1 tokens that ping on death for a bit of extra incidental reach. The deck plays as a burn-and-pump machine that wants to recur its best removal spells with Ogre Battlecaster while using direct damage to buff its creatures each turn.
tips: Attack to recast graveyard spells
Target face with Ogre Battlecaster recasts
Tap Thermo-Alchemist each turn
Chain damage to pump Spitfire/Pyreling
Let Devil tokens die for extra pings

decks.description: This is another Ogre Battlecaster-centered burn deck, but built with more direct removal-style burn spells like Searing Spear, Cone of Flame, and Viashino Pyromancer's ETB ping, which Ogre Battlecaster can rebuy from the graveyard on attack for extra damage and a size boost. Blisterspit Gremlin again provides a repeatable, untapping pinger whenever you cast a noncreature spell, stacking incidental damage that triggers Chandra's Spitfire and Chandra's Pyreling to snowball combat damage, while Wildfire Elemental turns every burn spell into a team-wide pump. Coalborn Entity gives you a cheap repeatable ping for reach against tokens and planeswalkers as well as players. The overall plan is consistent: use small, efficient burn spells to whittle down the opponent and their blockers while simultaneously growing your creatures' power through the Spitfire/Pyreling/Wildfire trio, then close with recursive value from Ogre Battlecaster.
tips: Rebuy burn with Ogre Battlecaster
Chain noncreature spells for Blisterspit
Ping to pump Spitfire/Pyreling
Use Cone of Flame on multiple blockers
Wildfire Elemental turns burn into pump

decks.description: This deck is a classic go-wide Goblin tribal build that wants to flood the board with cheap token generators and then close the game with Krenko, Mob Boss, whose tap ability creates X 1/1 Goblins equal to your current Goblin count, meaning even a modest board of 3-4 Goblins explodes into a massive army in one activation. Hordeling Outburst and Dragon Fodder cheaply add bodies to fuel that count, Battle Squadron scales its power and toughness with your total creature count so it grows for free as the board fills up, and Volley Veteran's ETB damage also scales with how many Goblins you control, turning a wide board into a removal spell. Burn Bright then pumps the whole team by +2/+0 for a lethal alpha strike, while Goblin Grenade sacrifices a Goblin to deal a chunky 5 damage, giving you reach even if combat gets clogged. This is a token-swarm aggro deck that snowballs quickly once Krenko starts firing.
tips: Build board before firing Krenko
Untap Krenko for exponential goblins
Burn Bright for alpha strike
Sac extra Goblin to Goblin Grenade
Volley Veteran scales with Goblin count

decks.description: This Goblin deck shifts from pure explosive swarming toward tempo and unblockability, powered by Goblin Warchief, which discounts your Goblin spells by 1 and gives all your Goblins haste, letting Hordeling-style token makers and Volley Veteran hit the board swinging immediately. Ib Halfheart, Goblin Tactician turns bad blocks against you into free removal, sacrificing a blocked Goblin to deal 4 damage to everything blocking it, and can also convert two Mountains into two more Goblin tokens when you need fresh bodies. Frenzied Goblin and Goblin Trailblazer help push damage through with menace and a pay-to-not-block effect, while Volley Veteran's ETB damage scales with your Goblin count just like in the other list. Goblin Rally and Dragon Fodder keep the token count climbing, Burn Bright provides a team-wide pump for the finishing attack, and Goblin Grenade offers reach by sacrificing a Goblin for 5 damage. Overall this is a hasty, tempo-oriented Goblin aggro deck that punishes blocks and pushes through evasive damage rather than relying on one big Krenko turn.
tips: Warchief grants haste, swing immediately
Bait blocks for Ib Halfheart value
Push damage with Trailblazer/Frenzied Goblin
Sac Mountains late for more Goblins
Burn Bright to finish

decks.description: This deck is a straightforward tribal Goblin aggro pile that wants to flood the board with token Goblins and then finish opponents off with burn and sacrifice payoffs. Dragon Fodder and Goblin Rally-style effects aren't here, but Dragon Fodder itself makes two bodies, and Muxus, Goblin Grandee is the huge payoff card, dropping potentially several Goblins for free straight onto the battlefield when it enters. The deck's real synergy is sacrificing its own Goblins for value: Arms Dealer turns any Goblin into a 4-damage removal spell by sacrificing it, and Goblin Grenade requires sacrificing a Goblin to deal 5 damage to any target, meaning small token Goblins from Dragon Fodder become disposable fuel for direct damage. Volley Veteran scales its enters-the-battlefield damage off however many Goblins you already control, rewarding you for building a wide board before playing it, and Burn Bright's team-wide +2/+0 turns a swarm of 1/1 tokens into a lethal alpha strike.
tips: Sequence tokens before Volley Veteran
Sacrifice extra tokens to Arms Dealer/Grenade
Cast Muxus with a full library
Save Burn Bright for lethal swing

decks.description: This build leans even harder into the Goblin token swarm plan, with Goblin Rabblemaster and Ardoz, Cobbler of War both generating and pumping tokens turn after turn. Goblin Rabblemaster forces your other Goblins to attack while making a new hasty 1/1 Goblin every combat and growing itself with each attacking Goblin, while Ardoz gives every entering creature (including those tokens) +2/+0, meaning tokens from Hordeling Outburst, Goblin Rally, and Dragon Fodder all come in swinging much harder the turn they're made. Torch Courier can sacrifice itself to hand haste to a freshly cast creature, letting you generate immediate pressure from anything that lacks it, and Goblin Grenade again turns a spare token into 5 direct damage. The overall plan is to dump the board with 1/1 tokens as fast as possible, buff them all at once with Burn Bright or Ardoz's ETB trigger, and close games quickly through sheer numbers rather than any single big threat.

Alpha strike with Burn Bright

decks.description: This is a classic Raid deck built to attack first with a cheap threat, then flood in bigger payoffs once the Raid condition (having attacked this turn) is already met. Kari Zev, Skyship Raider is the ideal opener since her attack trigger creates an extra attacking token every combat, virtually guaranteeing Raid is active for everything played afterward. Cards like Swaggering Corsair, Rigging Runner, and War-Name Aspirant all enter with a bonus +1/+1 counter if you've already attacked, while Storm Fleet Pyromancer and Firecannon Blast punish opponents with bonus damage under the same condition (Firecannon Blast jumps from 3 to a whopping 6 damage). Daring Piracy adds a free hasty menace token every combat to keep Raid turned on even on turns when your other creatures are tapped out, and Goblin Oriflamme's blanket +1/+0 to attackers pushes extra damage through once the swarm is established.
tips: Attack with a small creature first
Kari Zev triggers Raid every turn
Cast Raid creatures post-combat for counters
Save Firecannon Blast for 6 damage
Use Daring Piracy to guarantee Raid

decks.description: This version of Raid pivots toward a discard-and-burn subtheme layered on top of the usual attack-first plan. Glint-Horn Buccaneer punishes every discard with 1 damage to each opponent and can loot repeatedly while attacking, and it combos directly with the deck's cycle of loot creatures — Immersturm Raider, Keldon Raider, and Plundering Predator all let you discard a card to draw one when they enter, each discard pinging opponents if Glint-Horn Buccaneer is out. Bag of Holding doubles as a repeatable loot engine, drawing and discarding a card each turn (again feeding Glint-Horn Buccaneer's damage trigger) while exiling discarded cards to protect against graveyard hate, with the option to later return them all to hand. Meanwhile the Raid creatures (Rigging Runner, Swaggering Corsair, War-Name Aspirant) and Firecannon Blast still want you attacking first to maximize their bonuses, and Lightning Axe offers cheap removal that can even be paid for with a discard instead of mana.
tips: Land Glint-Horn Buccaneer early
Loot with Raiders to ping opponents
Use Bag of Holding each turn
Attack before casting Raid creatures
Pay Lightning Axe with discard

decks.description: This Raid build follows the same core plan of attacking first to unlock bonuses on Rigging Runner, Swaggering Corsair, War-Name Aspirant, Mardu Heart-Piercer, and Firecannon Blast, but adds tools to keep the attack going and generate late-game value. Axgard Cavalry grants haste to any creature, letting you push out a freshly cast threat to attack immediately and set up Raid for your other spells that same turn, while Fervent Strike gives a creature +1/+0, first strike, and haste to enable a similar aggressive tempo play or push through an otherwise risky attacker. Brazen Cannonade rewards continued aggression twice over: it burns opponents for 2 whenever an attacking creature dies, and if Raid is active it exiles the top card of your library for you to play, effectively drawing extra action just for attacking. Daring Piracy's recurring hasty menace token keeps the Raid condition active even in slower turns, tying the whole deck's card advantage back into simply attacking every turn.

Axgard Cavalry to enable surprise attacks
Keep attacking for Brazen Cannonade value

Daring Piracy guarantees Raid trigger

decks.description: This Raid deck emphasizes creatures that reward attacking with their own built-in combat pump rather than relying purely on ETB counters. Borderland Marauder and Brazen Wolves each get +2/+0 whenever they attack, turning small bodies into serious threats the moment combat starts, and Ordeal of Purphoros stacks +1/+1 counters onto an attacking creature until it has three, at which point it sacrifices itself to deal 3 damage to any target — a slow-building but guaranteed removal or reach engine tied to repeated attacks. The Raid creatures (Rigging Runner, Swaggering Corsair, War-Name Aspirant, Storm Fleet Pyromancer) still want an early attacker to unlock their bonuses, and Brazen Cannonade again punishes attacker deaths with 2 damage to the opponent while rewarding continued Raid activity with extra card plays. Daring Piracy's free hasty token remains the glue that keeps the Raid condition satisfied turn after turn, letting the deck's payoffs consistently come online.

Use Daring Piracy to enable Raid

Cannonade punishes trades

decks.description: This colorless-heavy Eldrazi deck plays a grindy ramp-and-value game, using devoid creatures and colorless mana fixing to smooth out its curve while generating incremental card and mana advantage. Brood Monitor, Blisterpod, and Scion Summoner all produce sacrificeable Eldrazi Scion tokens that can be cracked for colorless mana, ramping toward the deck's bigger threats like World Breaker and Endbringer. World Breaker doubles as removal and recursion: casting it exiles an opposing artifact, enchantment, or land, and later it can be brought back from the graveyard by sacrificing a land, letting you reuse its exile effect. Endbringer is a repeatable value engine on its own, untapping during opponents' untap steps so it can ping for 1 damage, lock down an attacker or blocker, or draw a card every turn depending on what's needed. Titan's Presence and Unnatural Aggression provide conditional but powerful removal, while Ancient Stirrings and Ash Barrens smooth land and colorless-card draws to keep the ramp plan consistent.
tips: Ramp with sacrificeable Scion tokens
Recur World Breaker via graveyard
Use Endbringer every turn cycle

Save Titan's Presence for big threats

decks.description: This green tribal deck builds around Apes and Monkeys with a secondary theme of punishing artifacts, all while ramping toward a big trample or fight finisher. Kibo, Uktabi Prince is the centerpiece: it forces every player to make a sacrificeable Banana artifact token, and whenever an opponent's artifact (including that very token) dies, every Ape or Monkey you control gets a +1/+1 counter, meaning Kibo's attack trigger that forces a sacrifice directly fuels your own team's growth. Uktabi Orangutan adds more artifact destruction to keep that engine firing, and Hooting Mandrills leans on Delve to come down as a cheap, hard-hitting trampler once the graveyard has filled up. Towering Gibbon scales its power off the biggest mana value creature you control, so ramping into Kibo or Hooting Mandrills also inflates Gibbon's stats, and Ram Through lets any of your creatures (ideally a trampler like Silverback Shaman or Hooting Mandrills) fight down an opposing blocker while pushing leftover damage to its controller.
tips: Attack with Kibo to force sacrifice
Destroy artifacts for counter triggers
Delve out Hooting Mandrills late
Ramp mana value to grow Gibbon
Ram Through with tramplers

decks.description: This green Landfall deck wants to maximize the number of times a land enters the battlefield each turn, turning that simple event into repeated value and damage. Rampaging Baloths creates a 4/4 Beast every single land drop, while Primeval Herald and Oashra Cultivator dig up extra basics to trigger landfall more than once per turn, and Rampaging Growth both ramps and briefly animates the fetched land into a 4/3 hasty attacker. Snapping Gnarlid and Canopy Baloth turn every land drop into a temporary combat pump, letting creatures punch above their size on the turn they matter most, while Zendikar's Roil and Khalni Heart Expedition convert lands into tokens and long-term ramp respectively. Courser of Kruphix smooths this whole plan by letting you play lands straight off the top of your library (extending your land drops) while also gaining life off each landfall trigger, and Turntimber Basilisk's landfall ability to force a blocker combines with its deathtouch to reliably snipe down whatever the opponent puts in the way.
tips: Chain extra land drops for landfall
Use Primeval Herald/Cultivator to fetch
Force blocks with Turntimber Basilisk
Courser lets you play top-deck lands
Time land drops before combat pumps

decks.description: This Landfall build is built around a couple of huge single-card payoffs that reward extra land drops rather than a wide spread of small triggers. Avenger of Zendikar creates a 0/1 Plant token for every land you control when it enters, and its landfall ability can then put a +1/+1 counter on every one of those Plants each time a land enters afterward, so ramping into it with a full board of lands can immediately flood the field with a wall of growing tokens. Baloth Woodcrasher turns every land drop into a massive +4/+4 and trample buff, making it a serious combat threat the instant you play an extra land, and Groundswell doubles down on the same idea by upgrading from +2/+2 to +4/+4 if you've already had a landfall trigger that turn. Fertilid and Rampaging Growth both fetch extra lands to reliably enable these landfall payoffs, while Turntimber Basilisk again offers a repeatable removal-adjacent ability by forcing bad blocks with deathtouch backup.
tips: Land Avenger of Zendikar with many lands
Trigger extra landfall for Woodcrasher
Save Groundswell for double landfall turn

Force blocks with Turntimber Basilisk

decks.description: This mono-green pile is built entirely around stacking +1/+1 counters onto a handful of hydras and then abusing counter-doubling and counter-redirection effects to close games fast. Benevolent Hydra is the centerpiece: its replacement effect means any counters that would go on your other creatures instead arrive as one extra, so every Fertilid trigger, Servant of the Scale death trigger, or Hunter's Edge cast is quietly buffed. Hydra's Growth is the other star; slapped onto Primordial-style growth targets like Clockwork Hydra or Feral Hydra, it doubles that creature's counters every upkeep, turning a modest 4/4 into an enormous threat within a couple of turns. Ramp pieces like Ilysian Caryatid (which taps for two mana once you have a power-4-or-greater creature) and Fertilid smooth the mana while Adventurous Impulse and Scale the Heights dig for lands and threats. The deck doesn't have much interaction beyond Hunter's Edge and Soul's Might, so it wants to just plant a hydra, protect it, and grow it until it's lethal.
tips: Land Hydra's Growth on your biggest hydra
Play Benevolent Hydra early to boost future counters
Use Fertilid to ramp then keep growing
Protect your hydra, it's your win condition
Soul's Might as a surprise finisher

decks.description: Like its companion pool, this deck is a mono-green counters deck, but it leans harder into exponential growth via Primordial Hydra, which doubles its own counters every upkeep and gains trample once it hits ten counters, snowballing from a starting X into a game-ending threat in just a couple of turns if left unchecked. Benevolent Hydra again warps the math for the rest of the board, turning single-counter effects from Kujar Seedsculptor or Pridemalkin into two counters, and Pridemalkin's ability grants trample to anything holding a counter, meaning even small buffed creatures become evasive threats. Hydra's Growth can go on Primordial Hydra itself for a truly absurd doubling-of-a-doubling, or onto another creature to create a second giant threat. Titanic Brawl gets cheaper against your own countered-up creatures and lets you use that size advantage to remove problematic blockers, while Ordeal of Nylea offers both battlefield pressure and a late-game ramp payoff.
tips: Prioritize Primordial Hydra survival
Stack Hydra's Growth on your biggest doubler
Use Pridemalkin for cheap trample enablers

Benevolent Hydra multiplies every counter trigger

decks.description: This is a ramp deck focused on casting the biggest green creatures as early as possible and then making them even bigger. Runadi, Behemoth Caller is the key payoff: any creature spell with mana value 5 or more enters with bonus +1/+1 counters equal to its mana value minus 4, and any creature with three or more counters gains haste, so a freshly-cast Colossal Dreadmaw or Woodborn Behemoth can often attack the turn it lands. Towering Gibbon's power scales off the highest mana value creature you control, meaning it naturally becomes huge alongside your top-end. Mana dorks like Naga Vitalist and land-fixers like Ondu Giant and Gift of the Gargantuan accelerate you toward these fatties, while Overgrowth turbocharges a single land into a huge mana source. Enlarge and Pounce provide reach and removal, letting a single oversized creature swing for a huge chunk of damage or trade up against anything the opponent plays.

Cast 5+ MV creatures for Runadi's counters and haste
Enlarge a creature for a haste-like alpha strike

Towering Gibbon scales with your biggest threat

decks.description: A near-twin of its companion, this deck also ramps into oversized green threats and uses Runadi, Behemoth Caller to hand out bonus +1/+1 counters and haste to expensive creatures the turn they enter. Engulfing Slagwurm rewards you for blocking or being blocked by turning any combat into a two-for-one, destroying the opposing creature and gaining life equal to its toughness, which is brutal against aggressive decks. Paradise Druid provides safe, hexproof-while-untapped mana acceleration, and Mammoth Spider adds a reach blocker to handle fliers that would otherwise dodge these ground-pounders. As with the other Gigantic build, Overgrowth juices a single land for double green, letting you deploy giant threats ahead of schedule, while Enlarge and Prey Upon let your biggest creature either finish the game outright or brawl profitably with an opposing blocker.
tips: Ramp with Paradise Druid, keep it untapped for hexproof
Engulfing Slagwurm blocks profitably, always trades up
Runadi turns expensive creatures into instant threats
Overgrowth accelerates toward your top end
Mammoth Spider covers your flying weakness

decks.description: This is a classic Elf tribal go-wide deck that generates a stream of 1/1 Elf Warrior tokens and then pumps the whole team for a big alpha strike. Elvish Warmaster is the engine, popping out a free token the first time any other Elf enters each turn, which combos naturally with Dwynen's Elite (makes a token if you control another Elf) and Elderleaf Mentor (makes a token on entry) to flood the board quickly. Ivy Lane Denizen turns every one of those green creature entries into a +1/+1 counter somewhere on the board, and once you have a wide enough army, Might of the Masses (pump equal to creature count) or Elvish Warmaster's own late-game anthem-plus-deathtouch ability turn a mass of small tokens into a lethal swing. Band Together offers a way to fight down blockers using two of your creatures' combined power. The deck's core plan is simple: flood the board with cheap Elves, then finish with a mass pump spell.
tips: Sequence Elves to chain Warmaster and Dwynen's Elite triggers
Ivy Lane Denizen counters stack fast with tokens
Save Might of the Masses for lethal
Warmaster's deathtouch ability wins wide boards
Band Together clears key blockers

decks.description: Another Elf tribal token-swarm deck, this build swaps in Imperious Perfect as its centerpiece: a static +1/+1 anthem for every other Elf plus a repeatable mana sink that makes another 1/1 Elf Warrior token each turn. Combined with Dwynen's Elite (free token off another Elf) and Elderleaf Mentor (token on entry), the board fills up quickly with buffed tokens. Ivy Lane Denizen converts every green creature entering into a +1/+1 counter for any target, letting you concentrate extra toughness or power where it matters most, and Ghirapur Guide can push a key attacker through small blockers once the board state gets crowded. As before, Might of the Masses scales with your total creature count for a explosive finishing pump, and Roots of Wisdom helps refill your hand with lands or Elves in the grind. This deck wants to go wide behind Imperious Perfect's anthem and end the game with a big Might of the Masses swing.

Use its tap ability every turn for more tokens
Ghirapur Guide pushes damage past small blockers

Roots of Wisdom recurs key Elves or lands

decks.description: This Elf deck focuses less on raw anthem effects and more on token generation feeding a single scaling threat, Woodland Champion, which gets a +1/+1 counter for every token that enters at once. Wolverine Riders is a strong token engine on its own, making a 1/1 Elf Warrior every upkeep (yours and your opponents') and gaining you life whenever another Elf enters, while Lys Alana Huntmaster converts every Elf spell you cast into another token. Ivy Lane Denizen again turns green creature entries into flexible +1/+1 counters, and Elderleaf Mentor and Dwynen's Elite add to the token count. Instruments of War lets you name Elf (or Warrior) for a cheap anthem effect that can come down at instant speed to blindside combat math. With enough tokens hitting the board simultaneously, Woodland Champion can balloon in size very quickly, and Might of the Masses or Band Together close out the game once the board is sufficiently wide.

Wolverine Riders gives free tokens and lifegain

decks.description: This build shifts the Elf shell toward maximizing value from tokens rather than tribal synergy alone. Woodland Champion again grows off every token entering simultaneously, and Dutiful Replicator can copy any token you control (an Elf Warrior, a Food, whatever you've made), effectively doubling your best token for extra value or an extra Woodland Champion trigger. Presence of Gond and Elven Bow both turn creatures into repeatable or one-shot Elf Warrior generators, feeding both Woodland Champion and Ivy Lane Denizen's counter-granting trigger. Wildborn Preserver rewards you for casting more non-Human creatures by letting you pump it with mana as those creatures enter, becoming a growing threat parallel to Woodland Champion. Overcome caps off the game plan, turning a wide, token-heavy board into a lethal trampling alpha strike after a long token-generation game.

decks.description: This Wolves tribal deck builds a pack of Wolf and Werewolf tokens and creatures, boosted by Arlinn, Voice of the Pack's static ability that gives every Wolf or Werewolf you control an extra +1/+1 counter as it enters, meaning tokens from Ferocious Pup, Wolfkin Bond, and Wolfwillow Haven's sacrifice ability all come in as 3/3s instead of 2/2s. Packsong Pup snowballs on its own as long as you control another Wolf or Werewolf, growing every combat, and Wicked Wolf doubles as a removal spell on a stick by fighting an opposing creature on entry, then later using Food sacrifice to gain indestructibility and extra counters. Wolf's Quarry and Fierce Witchstalker generate Food tokens that both fuel Wicked Wolf's protective ability and provide incidental lifegain. The overall plan is to flood the board with Arlinn-buffed Wolves, use Wicked Wolf and Prey Upon to pick off blockers, then swing with a pack that's bigger than it looks.

decks.description: This Wolves build focuses more on combat tricks and a repeatable damage engine than the companion list. Master of the Wild Hunt is the standout card: it makes a 2/2 Wolf every upkeep and can tap down all your untapped Wolves to deal their combined power to a single target, splitting the damage among them as you choose, which combos directly with Moonlight Hunt (every Wolf/Werewolf deals damage equal to its power to a chosen creature) for a similar but instant-speed removal effect that can wipe out a problem blocker or finish off a player's key creature in one shot. Arlinn, Voice of the Pack again buffs incoming Wolf and Werewolf tokens with an extra counter, and Young Wolf and Howlgeist's undying abilities mean cheap creatures come back bigger after trading in combat. Howl of the Hunt is a flash pump-and-untap trick that can ambush attackers or push through extra damage unexpectedly. This deck wants to build a Wolf army, then use its mass-damage abilities to clear the way for lethal attacks.
tips: Master of the Wild Hunt taps team for burst removal
Moonlight Hunt as instant-speed wolf removal
Undying wolves trade favorably in combat
Arlinn boosts every Wolf token that enters
Flash Howl of the Hunt to ambush attackers

decks.description: This deck is a mono-green tribal Wolves swarm that wants to flood the board with 2/2 Wolf tokens and then pump the whole team for a lethal alpha strike. Arlinn, Voice of the Pack makes every Wolf and Werewolf you make enter with an extra +1/+1 counter, so token generators like Predator's Howl, Ferocious Pup, and Feed the Pack are all putting 3/3s onto the battlefield instead of vanilla 2/2s while she's out. Predator's Howl and Feed the Pack both reward a sacrifice-heavy or attrition-heavy game: Predator's Howl's Morbid clause triples its token output to three Wolves if a creature died that turn, and Feed the Pack lets you sac a nontoken creature at end of turn to make X tokens equal to its toughness, meaning a beefed-up Sporeback Wolf or a counter-loaded Mild-Mannered Librarian can go out generating a small army. Kessig Cagebreakers is the other payoff, turning every creature card sitting in your graveyard into an attacking Wolf token the moment it swings, so letting some of your own Wolves trade away earlier in the game actually fuels a bigger Cagebreakers attack later. Spectral Hunt-Caller's late-game mana sink gives the whole board +1/+1 and trample to close games once you've gone wide.
tips: Play Arlinn before token makers
Sac fodder into Feed the Pack
Let creatures die for Morbid/Cagebreakers
Dump graveyard, then swing with Cagebreakers
Use Spectral Hunt-Caller to finish

decks.description: This build leans further into the anthem-and-flash side of the Wolves plan than raw token count. Arlinn, Voice of the Pack still buffs every Wolf/Werewolf that enters, and Nightpack Ambusher doubles as both a lord (+1/+1 to other Wolves/Werewolves) and a repeatable token engine that fires every end step you don't cast a spell, rewarding you for holding up mana and passing with flash creatures like Briarpack Alpha and Bounding Wolf in hand instead of casting sorcery-speed spells. Those flash creatures (Briarpack Alpha's +2/+2 combat trick, Bounding Wolf's reach body, and the flash Aura Howl of the Hunt, which untaps a Wolf/Werewolf it enchants while giving +2/+2 and vigilance) let you ambush combat or blowout an alpha strike while still keeping Nightpack Ambusher's token trigger alive. Wolfwillow Haven ramps you into Spectral Hunt-Caller's expensive team pump, and Prey Upon lets a buffed Wolf clear away a blocker. The overall gameplan is patient board development into a big flash-backed combat turn rather than pure token flooding.
tips: Hold mana for Nightpack Ambusher tokens
Flash in tricks during combat
Untap attackers with Howl of the Hunt
Ramp with Wolfwillow Haven
Stack Arlinn counters before tokens

decks.description: This deck's whole identity revolves around getting a creature with power 4 or greater onto the battlefield and then leaning on every card that checks for it. Rhonas the Indomitable is both a payoff and a bottleneck: it can't attack or block without another 4-power creature around, but once one is out it becomes a nearly unkillable deathtouch threat and can also pump another creature's power with its activated ability to help hit that Ferocious threshold. Ilysian Caryatid and Colossal Majesty both scale with Ferocious being active, turning a single big body into a mana engine and a card-draw engine at the same time, while Challenger Troll and Ornery Dilophosaur punish blockers once you're big. Savage Punch and Roar of Challenge are built to close games off a fight or a forced-block scenario, getting extra strength or indestructibility specifically when Ferocious is online. Rampaging Growth doubles as ramp and, for one turn, an actual 4/3 body that can satisfy Ferocious itself in a pinch.
tips: Prioritize a 4-power creature early
Rhonas needs another big body
Ferocious powers Caryatid and Majesty
Use Rampaging Growth as a body

decks.description: This version doubles down on cost reduction and team-wide payoffs once Ferocious is active. Goreclaw, Terror of Qal Sisma discounts every big creature spell you cast by {2} and, once it attacks, gives every power-4-or-greater creature you control +1/+1 and trample, turning your whole army of oversized bodies into an unblockable-feeling swarm. Nessian Hornbeetle grows every combat as long as another big creature is around, snowballing into its own Ferocious enabler over time, while Ilysian Caryatid and Colossal Majesty again convert a single big threat into extra mana and extra cards. Challenger Troll keeps your fatties from getting gang-blocked, and Savage Punch/Roar of Challenge again spike in value specifically when Ferocious is online, either pumping a fighter before combat or forcing through a huge indestructible attacker. Rosethorn Halberd is a cheap way to push a non-Human creature over the power-4 threshold if your draw is light on big bodies.

decks.description: This build is the most combat-trick-and-protection-focused Ferocious list, aiming to keep a single evasive power-4 threat alive and unblocked. Prowling Serpopard makes all your creature spells uncounterable, protecting your Ferocious enablers from countermagic, while Challenger Troll and Bristling Boar both stop your big creatures from being gang-blocked once they're swinging. Ornery Dilophosaur gets a huge deathtouch-boosted attack step whenever a 4-power creature is around, effectively threatening to eat any blocker for free, and Hammer of Ruin adds +2/+0 plus a way to blow up an opposing Equipment on combat damage. As in the other Ferocious builds, Ilysian Caryatid and Colossal Majesty scale off the same power-4 condition to give you extra mana and cards, and Savage Punch/Roar of Challenge again get bigger specifically when Ferocious triggers, letting you fight away or force through blockers for a lethal swing.
tips: Protect creatures with Serpopard

decks.description: This list mixes the standard Ferocious payoffs with a couple of unusual scaling threats. Towering Gibbon's power equals the greatest mana value among your creatures, so it can become a genuine Ferocious enabler on its own if you're casting expensive spells, and Creeperhulk's activated ability can turn any creature you control into a temporary 5/5 trampler, instantly satisfying the power-4 requirement for everything else in the deck on demand. Once Ferocious is active, Ilysian Caryatid, Colossal Majesty, Frontier Mastodon, Challenger Troll, and Drowsing Tyrannodon all flip on simultaneously, giving you mana, cards, a counter, evasion, and a surprise attacker all off the same condition. Bonders' Enclave gives this build a repeatable late-game card draw land that's also gated behind having a power-4 creature, and Kitesail can push any ground creature into the air to close out a game once your board is stabilized. Savage Punch and Roar of Challenge round out the removal/finisher suite exactly as in the other Ferocious decks.

decks.description: This deck builds a wide board of counter-covered Insects and then converts those counters directly into extra tokens each turn. Iridescent Hornbeetle is the centerpiece: at end step it makes a 1/1 Insect token for every +1/+1 counter you placed on any creature that turn, so cards like Ironshell Beetle, Duskshell Crawler, and Hunter's Edge that simply drop a single +1/+1 counter as an ETB or effect are also quietly building your token count. Duskshell Crawler additionally gives every counter-bearing creature trample, so your growing Insects and Swarm Shambler (which itself makes a token whenever an opponent targets one of your counter creatures) start punching through blockers instead of getting chumped. Moldgraf Millipede turns self-mill from Crawling Sensation and Relentless Pursuit into a snowballing counters payoff, since it counts creature cards in your graveyard when it enters, and Crawling Sensation's land-mill trigger is another independent source of 1/1 Insect tokens. The overall plan is grindy, counter-driven go-wide value rather than a single explosive combo turn.

decks.description: This deck is built around Zask, Skittering Swarmlord recurring your Insects out of both hand and graveyard. When another Insect you control dies, Zask puts it on the bottom of your library and mills two cards instead of letting it just sit in the graveyard, which keeps refilling your graveyard for Undergrowth cards like Kraul Harpooner and Kraul Foragers (both of which get better the more creature cards are in your graveyard) while also letting you replay lands and Insect spells straight from the graveyard over time. Crawling Sensation's land-mill trigger and Moldgraf Millipede's self-mill both feed that same graveyard, and Circuit Mender smooths the curve with life gain on entry and a card on exit, cushioning the attrition. Nantuko Cultivator lets you convert excess lands directly into card advantage and a bigger body. This is a grindy, incremental value deck that wants long games where Zask's graveyard recursion and Undergrowth threshold effects keep generating more resources than the opponent.
tips: Zask recurs dead Insects

decks.description: This deck combines the counters-into-tokens engine from Iridescent Hornbeetle and Nessian Hornbeetle with a graveyard-based scavenge finisher. Iridescent Hornbeetle turns every +1/+1 counter you place in a turn (from Ironshell Beetle's ETB, Hunter's Edge, or Phantom Nantuko's activated ability) into a 1/1 Insect token at end step, while Nessian Hornbeetle keeps growing itself once another big creature is on board. Phantom Nantuko is a durable centerpiece since damage to it just removes a counter instead of killing it outright, letting it tank hits while still counting toward your counters-matter payoffs. The real late-game swing comes from Deadbridge Goliath and Drudge Beetle's Scavenge abilities: once either dies and hits the graveyard, you can exile it to dump a huge pile of +1/+1 counters (equal to its power) onto a creature, and thanks to Iridescent Hornbeetle that single Scavenge activation can also convert straight into a wave of Insect tokens the same turn. Crawling Sensation and Relentless Pursuit fill the graveyard to set up these Scavenge triggers ahead of time.

decks.description: This build pairs Zask, Skittering Swarmlord's graveyard recursion with land-based value and a couple of unusual mana sinks. Zask mills two cards and recycles any Insect that dies back to the bottom of your library, continually restocking both your hand (via cast-from-graveyard) and your graveyard, which directly sets up Quarry Beetle, whose ETB returns a land straight from that graveyard back onto the battlefield for free extra ramp. Rampaging Growth doubles as ramp and a temporary 4/3 attacker, and Giant Caterpillar offers a sacrifice outlet that turns into a delayed flying Insect token, giving you a way to dodge removal or push through evasive damage later. Kraul Warrior's late-game pump and Zask's own deathtouch-granting ability let a single Insect punch above its weight in combat once the mid-game mana is available. Crawling Sensation keeps land cards flowing into the graveyard to feed both Zask's recursion loop and Quarry Beetle, making this a steady, land-and-graveyard-value grinder rather than an explosive aggro deck.

decks.description: This is a colorless artifact ramp package built around the classic Urza's Tower/Power-Plant/Mine land cycle, which pumps out massive amounts of colorless mana once all three land types are in play. That mana is funneled into big-swing artifact payoffs like Karn Liberated, whose -3 can simply exile any problematic permanent and whose ultimate ends the game outright, and Walking Ballista, a scalable threat that can enter huge or be built up over time and then pinged apart for direct damage. Utility artifacts like Expedition Map and Planar Atlas smooth out the mana base and help you actually assemble the Tower/Mine/Power-Plant trio, while Meteor Golem and Universal Solvent give you clunky but unconditional answers to anything an opponent plays. Golem Artisan and Locthwain Gargoyle turn your artifact creatures into evasive finishers once you have mana to spare, and Runed Servitor and Alchemist's Vial provide small value or disruption along the way. The deck's core interaction is straightforward: the Urza lands combo together to produce excess colorless mana, which lets you deploy Walking Ballista at a large size or activate it repeatedly, and that same mana surplus lets Karn Liberated's abilities come online ahead of curve.
